content.text: A policy initiative (the ARM resource type is policySetDefinition) is a collection of policy definitions grouped under a single object that can be assigned together. Instead of assigning ten separate policies and managing ten compliance states independently, you define an initiative that references all ten definitions and assign the initiative once. A single initiative assignment generates individual compliance states for each member policy, plus an aggregate compliance state for the initiative as a whole. Every Azure subscription is automatically assigned two built-in initiatives: Azure Security Benchmark and Enable Microsoft Defender for Cloud.

attrs.alt: Policy initiative structure showing parameters, policy references, groups, and assignment layer

content.text: The initiative definition is the blueprint — it declares what policies are included, what parameters the initiative exposes, how those parameters map to individual policy parameters, and optionally how policies are grouped into logical control categories. The initiative assignment is the runtime object that binds the initiative to a scope and supplies parameter values.

An initiative definition is a JSON object that mirrors a policy definition's outer shape but replaces policyRule with policyDefinitions (an array of references) and optionally includes policyDefinitionGroups for labeling. Parameters declared at the initiative level can be wired into individual policy references, allowing a single initiative parameter to flow to multiple constituent policies.

# Create the initiative definition at management group scope
az policy set-definition create \
  --name corp-security-baseline \
  --display-name 'Corporate Security Baseline' \
  --definitions @initiative-defs.json \
  --params @initiative-params.json \
  --management-group mg-corp

# List all policy definitions included in the initiative
az policy set-definition show \
  --name corp-security-baseline \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --query 'policyDefinitions[].{PolicyId:policyDefinitionId,RefId:policyDefinitionReferenceId}' \
  -o table

# Assign the initiative to a management group
az policy assignment create \
  --name corp-baseline \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --policy-set-definition /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/corp-security-baseline \
  --params '{
    "logAnalyticsWorkspaceId": { "value": "/subscriptions/11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111/resourceGroups/rg-monitoring/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/law-corp" },
    "auditVmAgentEffect": { "value": "AuditIfNotExists" },
    "denyPublicStorageEffect": { "value": "Deny" }
  }'

# View initiative compliance summary
az policy state summarize \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --filter "policySetDefinitionId eq '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/corp-security-baseline'" \
  --query 'policyDefinitions[].{Policy:policyDefinitionId,Compliant:results.resourceDetails[0].count,NonCompliant:results.resourceDetails[1].count}'

Azure ships hundreds of built-in initiatives that implement compliance frameworks such as CIS Microsoft Azure Foundations, NIST SP 800-53, ISO 27001, PCI DSS, and FedRAMP. Built-in initiatives cannot be modified — they are Microsoft-managed and versioned. When you need to use a built-in but adjust a subset of its policies (e.g., disable a check that does not apply to your environment), you use initiative parameter overrides at assignment time to set those policy effects to Disabled.
CIS Microsoft Azure Foundations Benchmark v2.0

# Find built-in initiatives by category
az policy set-definition list \
  --query "[?policyType=='BuiltIn' && metadata.category=='Regulatory Compliance']
         .{Name:name,DisplayName:displayName}" \
  -o table

# Get the resource ID of a built-in initiative
az policy set-definition show \
  --name '1f3afdf9-d0c9-4c3d-847f-89da613e70a8' \
  --query 'id' -o tsv
# /providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/1f3afdf9-d0c9-4c3d-847f-89da613e70a8

# Assign the CIS benchmark with customized effect for a noisy check
az policy assignment create \
  --name cis-baseline \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --policy-set-definition /providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/06f19060-9e68-4070-92ca-f15cc126059e \
  --params '{
    "effect-12abcd34-5678-90ef-ghij-klmnopqrstuv": {"value": "Disabled"}
  }'
# The parameter name for each policy in a built-in initiative matches
# the format: effect-<policyDefinitionReferenceId>

A key design decision in initiative authoring is how parameters flow from the initiative level to individual policy definitions. There are three patterns: threading (wire an initiative parameter to the policy parameter), hardcoding (supply a fixed value in the initiative, not overridable at assignment time), and per-policy override at assignment (allowed for built-in initiatives).
One for all policies in the initiative
Exempt from specific policy within initiative via policyDefinitionReferenceId
Each assignment manages its own parameters
Shared parameters at initiative level, overridable at assignment
Version the initiative — one update propagates to all members
Natural fit — maps one initiative to one control framework
Exempting a Single Policy Within an Initiative

When a resource needs to be exempt from one specific policy inside an initiative assignment, you target the exemption to the policyDefinitionReferenceId of that policy. Without specifying a reference ID, the exemption covers all policies in the initiative for the resource. Scoping exemptions to individual policies is more precise and is the correct approach when, for example, a legacy VM cannot install the Log Analytics agent but all other policies in the baseline apply.

# Create an exemption for a specific policy within an initiative assignment
# Scenario: vm-legacy-01 cannot install the Log Analytics agent (it runs an old OS)
# but all other policies in the corp-baseline initiative should still apply

az policy exemption create \
  --name exempt-legacy-vm-agent \
  --resource /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-legacy/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-legacy-01 \
  --policy-assignment /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/corp-baseline \
  --policy-definition-reference-ids auditVmLawAgent \
  --exemption-category Waiver \
  --description 'vm-legacy-01 runs Windows Server 2008 R2 which does not support MMA agent' \
  --expires-on 2026-12-31T00:00:00Z

# List exemptions scoped to a specific initiative policy reference
az policy exemption list \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID \
  --query "[?policyAssignmentId contains 'corp-baseline' && policyDefinitionReferenceIds != null]
         .{Name:name,ReferenceIds:policyDefinitionReferenceIds,Expiry:expiresOn}" \
  -o table

# Check compliance state of the VM after exemption
az policy state list \
  --resource /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-legacy/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-legacy-01 \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'corp-baseline'" \
  --query '[].{RefId:policyDefinitionReferenceId,State:complianceState}' \
  -o table
# auditVmLawAgent: Exempt
# denyPublicStorage: Compliant (not exempted — still evaluated)

Custom initiatives should carry a version in their metadata to signal to consumers when the definition has changed. Updating the initiative definition (adding or removing policy references, changing parameter definitions) does not automatically update existing assignments — assignments continue to evaluate against the initiative version they were created with. After updating the initiative definition, update each assignment explicitly using az policy assignment update or Terraform apply. Adding a new policy definition to an initiative causes existing assignments to begin evaluating that policy immediately after the assignment is updated.

# Update the initiative definition to add a new policy reference (v1.1.0)
# 1. Retrieve the current initiative definition
az policy set-definition show \
  --name corp-security-baseline \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  > /tmp/current-initiative.json

# 2. Edit /tmp/current-initiative.json to:
#    - add the new policy reference to policyDefinitions[]
#    - bump metadata.version to '1.1.0'
#    - add any new parameters needed by the new policy

# 3. Update the initiative definition
az policy set-definition update \
  --name corp-security-baseline \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --definitions "$(jq '.policyDefinitions' /tmp/current-initiative.json)" \
  --params "$(jq '.parameters' /tmp/current-initiative.json)" \
  --metadata '{"version":"1.1.0","category":"Security"}'

# 4. Update the existing assignment to pick up the new policy
# If the new policy has a parameter, supply a value now
az policy assignment update \
  --name corp-baseline \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --set 'parameters.newPolicyEffect.value=AuditIfNotExists'

# 5. Trigger a compliance scan to evaluate all resources against the new policy
az policy state trigger-scan --management-group mg-corp

# 6. Verify the new policy reference is showing compliance state
az policy state summarize \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --filter "policySetDefinitionId contains 'corp-security-baseline'" \
  --query 'policyDefinitions[?contains(policyDefinitionId, `new-policy-def-name`)].results'

// Initiative-level compliance summary across subscriptions
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.policySetDefinitionId contains 'corp-security-baseline'
| summarize
    Compliant    = countif(properties.complianceState == 'Compliant'),
    NonCompliant = countif(properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'),
    Exempt       = countif(properties.complianceState == 'Exempt')
    by subscriptionId
| extend TotalEvaluated = Compliant + NonCompliant + Exempt
| extend CompliancePct  = round(100.0 * Compliant / TotalEvaluated, 1)
| order by CompliancePct asc

// Per-policy breakdown within the initiative
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.policySetDefinitionId contains 'corp-security-baseline'
| summarize
    NonCompliant = countif(properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant')
    by policyRef = tostring(properties.policyDefinitionReferenceId)
| where NonCompliant > 0
| order by NonCompliant desc

// Find resources that are non-compliant for ALL policies in the initiative
// (fully non-compliant resources — nothing is passing for them)
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.policySetDefinitionId contains 'corp-security-baseline'
| summarize
    Total       = count(),
    NonCompliant = countif(properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant')
    by resourceId
| where NonCompliant == Total
| project resourceId, NonCompliantPolicies = NonCompliant
| order by NonCompliantPolicies desc

// Track initiative compliance trend over time
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.policySetDefinitionId contains 'corp-security-baseline'
| where todatetime(properties.timestamp) > ago(30d)
| summarize
    Compliant    = countif(properties.complianceState == 'Compliant'),
    NonCompliant = countif(properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant')
    by bin(todatetime(properties.timestamp), 1d)
| extend CompliancePct = round(100.0 * Compliant / (Compliant + NonCompliant), 1)
| order by timestamp asc
Cannot tune behavior for dev vs prod without creating separate initiative definitions
Add effect parameters with allowedValues for every policy in the initiative
Different subscriptions need different workspaces but use the same initiative
Thread workspace ID as an initiative parameter, supply different values per assignment
Cannot target exemptions to individual policies within the initiative — exemption covers entire initiative
Always set policyDefinitionReferenceId on every entry; use a consistent naming scheme
Including policies that need managed identity in an initiative without assignment identity
DINE/Modify policies in the initiative never remediate — no identity to perform deployments
Add --mi-system-assigned and grant role to the assignment's managed identity after creating the assignment
Referencing policy definitions from different management group scopes
Initiative assignment fails to evaluate — policyDefinitionId not visible at the assignment scope
Ensure all referenced policies are defined at or above the initiative assignment scope
Never updating initiative assignments after definition update
New policies added to initiative are never evaluated — existing assignments use stale definition snapshot
Always update the assignment after updating the initiative definition

Policy initiatives group multiple policy definitions into a single assignable unit. They expose shared parameters at the initiative level that flow into individual policy parameters, enabling a single assignment to control behavior across many policies simultaneously. Built-in initiatives implement regulatory frameworks like CIS, NIST, and PCI DSS. Custom initiatives should parameterize all variable settings (especially effect values) so assignments can tune per-environment behavior without modifying the definition.
